sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Prehospital blood collection can reduce time to investigation and treatment after arrival, especially in sepsis, haemorrhage, ACS, or other time-sensitive presentations. Sampling should be targeted and should not delay transport or distract from resuscitation priorities.

sections.title: Common Blood Tubes
sections.content:
- Follow local pathology requirements for tube colour, order of draw, and special handling
- Only collect the samples that are likely to be useful for the receiving service and clinical picture
- If there is doubt about a tube requirement, label clearly and communicate what was collected rather than guessing

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Collect during cannulation or venepuncture when this can be done without delaying critical care or transport

2. Use the correct tubes and fill them adequately according to local pathology guidance

3. Mix additive tubes gently as required rather than shaking them

4. Label the specimens immediately at the patient side with the required identifiers

5. Store, hand over, and document the samples according to local protocol

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Mislabelling is one of the most important avoidable errors and can create serious patient safety risk
- Avoid sampling from a limb with running infusions where possible because dilution can invalidate results
- Haemolysis from poor technique can make some samples unusable
- Crossmatch and blood bank samples need especially careful identity handling and bedside labelling
